factual-accuracy.requirement: States combined federal+Ontario marginal tax rate at $95k income is 31%-32% or identifies bracket approximately $93k-$106k
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the combined federal and Ontario marginal tax rate at approximately CAD 95,000 is in the range of 31.48%, which falls within the required 31%-32% range.

The specific $310–$320 savings figure tied to a 31%–32% marginal rate is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2025 RRSP annual dollar limit is $32,490
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses RRSPs, RESPs, and Ontario marginal tax rates but never states the 2025 RRSP annual dollar limit. The specific figure "$32,490" does not appear anywhere in the response.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States RRSP contribution room is 18% of prior year earned income (lesser of percentage or dollar limit plus carry-forward)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses RRSP limits generally and notes spousal RRSP uses the contributor’s deduction room, but it never states the rule that RRSP contribution room is 18% of prior-year earned income (up to the annual dollar limit, plus carry-forward). factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates 18% of $95,000 yields approximately $17,000-$17,100 in new RRSP contribution room
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not compute 18% of $95,000 or state that it equals roughly $17,000–$17,100. It mentions RRSP contribution targets (e.g., $9,500–$14,000) but never ties RRSP room to 18% of income or provides the ~$17.1k figure.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG provides 20% grant on first $2,500 contributed per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Canada Education Savings Grant (CESG) matches 20% of the first $2,500 contributed per child annually in the “RESP and CESG Overview” section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG annual maximum is $500 per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the CESG matches 20% of the first $2,500 contributed per child annually “(up to $500/year)” and later reiterates “$500 CESG per child per year,” which directly gives the annual maximum.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG lifetime maximum is $7,200 per child
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the CESG has “a lifetime maximum of $7,200 per beneficiary,” which matches the required lifetime maximum per child.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains $5,000 contribution per child in one year can capture up to $1,000 CESG when unused grant room carried forward
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains CESG carry-forward and notes that catch-up contributions can be up to $5,000 per year to recoup missed CESG, and it also states that $5,000 annual RESP contributions for two kids maximize CESG. However, it does not explicitly connect “$5,000 per child in one year” to “up to $1,000 CESG” from unused grant room carried forward.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States to receive CESG at ages 16-17 must have contributed either $2,000 before year child turned 15 OR $100 in any four prior years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses CESG rules such as the 20% match on the first $2,500 per year, carry-forward catch-up, and that catch-up is available until age 17. However, it does not state the specific eligibility requirement for receiving CESG at ages 16–17 (the $2,000 before age 15 or $100 in any four prior years rule).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States RESP lifetime contribution limit is $50,000 per beneficiary
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states “CONTRIBUTION LIFETIME limit per child: $50,000” in the RESP section, which matches the required RESP lifetime contribution limit per beneficiary.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies $36,000 in contributions needed to maximize $7,200 CESG (since $7,200 divided by 20% equals $36,000)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions the $7,200 lifetime CESG maximum and the 20% match rate, but it never calculates or states that $36,000 in contributions are needed to maximize the $7,200 CESG. The specific $7,200 ÷ 20% = $36,000 figure is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes up to $14,000 additional contribution per child ($50,000 lifetime minus $36,000 for CESG) earns tax-deferred growth without additional grant
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the $50,000 lifetime RESP contribution limit and the $7,200 CESG lifetime maximum, but it does not note that up to $14,000 additional contribution per child ($50,000 minus $36,000 CESG-eligible contributions) can still grow tax-deferred without additional grant. The specific $36,000 CESG-eligible contribution cap and the resulting $14,000 figure are not stated.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States contributor claims RRSP deduction immediately at their marginal rate whether contribution goes to own RRSP or spousal RRSP
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that RRSP contributions provide an immediate tax deduction at the contributor’s marginal rate and explicitly notes that the contributor claims the deduction for spousal RRSP contributions as well as individual RRSP contributions (i.e., the deduction applies either way).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP account is owned by spouse and withdrawals after attribution period are taxed to spouse not contributor
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains that a spousal RRSP is registered in the other spouse’s (annuitant’s) name and that withdrawals are taxed as the annuitant spouse’s income. It also describes the 3-year attribution rule and implies that after this period, withdrawals are taxed to the spouse rather than the contributor.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States spousal RRSP 3-calendar-year attribution rule (withdrawal in contribution year or following 2 calendar years attributed back to contributor)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the CRA spousal RRSP “3-year attribution rule” and explains that withdrawals within three calendar years of the contributor’s last contribution are taxed back to the contributor, matching the contribution year or following two calendar years concept.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes minimum RRIF payments from spousal account are exempt from attribution (only excess over minimum can be attributed back)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the 3-year spousal RRSP attribution rule generally but does not mention the specific exception that minimum RRIF payments from a spousal account are exempt from attribution (only amounts above the minimum can be attributed). This RRIF-minimum exemption is not addressed anywhere in the spousal RRSP/RRIF discussion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP creates income-splitting opportunity if spouse expected to be in lower tax bracket during retirement
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains that a spousal RRSP enables income splitting by letting the higher-income spouse take the deduction now while withdrawals are taxed to the annuitant spouse, and it explicitly notes this is beneficial if the spouse is expected to be in a lower tax bracket in retirement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2025 TFSA annual contribution limit is $7,000

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains withdrawn amount from TFSA is added back to contribution room on January 1 of following calendar year

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise Canadian tax terms (RRSP RESP TFSA CESG RRIF attribution marginal rate) without generic language
